nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear to turbid slow-moving rivers, streams, and lake margins; often associated with submerged vegetation and marginal plant growth in highland plateau drainages

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, hornwort, water sprite)
floating plants for diffused light and refuge
open swimming areas for courtship display
driftwood or smooth rocks as cover

diet.notes: Omnivore with a strong herbivorous tendency; accepts quality flake, micro-pellets, spirulina-based foods, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, lettuce). Supplement with small live or frozen foods (daphnia, brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worms) to encourage conditioning and breeding. Wild fish graze on algae, detritus, small invertebrates, and plant matter. A varied diet high in vegetable matter supports long-term health.

goodWith: other Goodeids of similar size (Xenotoca eiseni, Zoogoneticus tequila)
small danios and rasboras tolerant of cooler water
avoidWith: large fast-moving fish that outcompete for food
tropical species requiring temperatures above 28°C (too warm for Skiffia)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Skiffia multipunctata; shows characteristic spotting pattern on male. Verify availability before use.

summary: A Critically Endangered Mexican Goodeid livebearer from the Lerma-Chapala basin, now maintained almost exclusively by conservation hobbyists. Males show attractive gold-and-black spotting; females are olive-brown. Trophotaenial placenta feeds developing fry, making breeding moderately demanding but deeply rewarding.
careNotes:
Skiffia multipunctata is a small Goodeid livebearer endemic to the Lerma-Chapala basin of central Mexico, particularly Lago de Chapala and associated drainages. The species is considered Critically Endangered or Extinct in the Wild by IUCN assessments due to catastrophic habitat loss from Lago de Chapala’s severe water-level declines, agricultural runoff, and introduced species. Captive populations maintained by conservation hobbyists represent the primary insurance against total extinction.

A minimum 60–75 L tank is generally recommended for a trio or small group, with a 60 cm footprint. Maintain cooler water than typical tropical fish — 18–24°C is ideal; sustained temperatures above 27°C stress the fish and reduce lifespan. Neutral to mildly alkaline, moderately hard water (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 10–25) mirrors highland Mexican conditions. Dense planting provides refuge and reduces inter-fish aggression; a mixed male-to-female ratio of 1:2 or 1:3 eases pressure on females.

Because of the conservation significance, hobbyists keeping this species are strongly encouraged to connect with studbook programs (e.g., GOODEID WORKING GROUP / GWG) and participate in coordinated breeding efforts. Avoid mixing with wild-type populations of other Goodeid species to prevent hybridisation. Males display a distinctive spot pattern and iridescent gold-and-black colouration during courtship; females are olive-grey with subdued spotting. Males can occasionally nip at females outside breeding; the recommended 2–3 females per male mitigates harassment. This species is rarely available in the general hobby trade and is typically obtained through specialist Goodeid keeper networks.
breedingNotes: Like all Goodeids, Skiffia multipunctata is a true viviparous livebearer: embryos develop internally via trophotaeniae — ribbon-like fetal outgrowths that absorb uterine secretions from the mother, providing a placenta-like nutrient connection. Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks at 22–24°C, with broods of 10–30 relatively large fry. Fry are born well-developed and free-swimming, capable of accepting micro-crushed flake, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and micro-worms from birth. No parental care is provided; females do not actively eat fry under well-fed conditions, but dense planting and a separate rearing container improve survival rates for conservation purposes. Conditioning pairs on a varied, protein-rich diet improves brood frequency. Males can exhibit persistent courtship pressure; a 1:2 male-to-female ratio is recommended. Females may produce 4–6 broods per year under good conditions. Interbirth interval is longer than Poeciliid livebearers due to the more resource-intensive trophotaenial gestation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Skiffia multipunctata
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided water parameters (temperature 18–26°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 100–250 mg/L), adult size (males to 6 cm, females to 7 cm), tank requirements, diet (omnivore, plant-heavy), breeding notes (trophotaeniae, gestation 6–8 weeks, 10–30 fry), conservation status (CR/EW), and native range (Lerma-Chapala basin, Mexico).

additional.name: FishBase — Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (to 7 cm TL), native range (Lerma-Chapala basin, Jalisco and Michoacan states, Mexico), IUCN status (Critically Endangered), temperature range (18–26°C), and diet classification. Confirmed species is listed as “Extinct in the Wild” in some regional assessments with captive populations in hobbyist studbooks.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Conservation status assessment: Critically Endangered (CR) with some regional authorities listing as Extinct in the Wild (EW). Threats documented: Lago de Chapala water-level reduction, agricultural pollution, invasive species (tilapia, common carp). Notes relevance of captive conservation programs including European and Mexican zoos and the Goodeid Working Group hobbyist network.

additional.name: Goodeid Working Group (GWG) — Species Profile: Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Specialist conservation hobbyist source. Confirmed studbook coordination, captive-population management guidelines, breeding recommendations (1:2 male-to-female ratio, cool water 20–24°C), and importance of genetic diversity maintenance across captive lines. Notes that this species is one of the GWG priority species for ex-situ conservation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Goodeid Livebearer Care
additional.notes: General Goodeid care article referencing Skiffia multipunctata as an example conservation species. Confirmed tropical temperature avoidance (keep below 26°C), omnivore diet with vegetable emphasis, trophotaenial reproduction biology, and recommendation to source fish from GWG-registered keepers rather than commercial trade.

fish.breedingNotes: True viviparous Goodeid with trophotaenial placentation; gestation 6–8 weeks at 22–24°C, 10–30 large fry per brood. Fry born free-swimming and large enough for micro-crushed flake and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Maintain 1:2 male-to-female ratio to reduce harassment. Condition breeders on varied diet with protein-rich live/frozen foods. Participating in GWG studbook programs strongly encouraged for conservation purposes.
